payload.formula_text: Subscriber Growth Rate = ((Current Quarter - Prior Quarter) / Prior Quarter) × 100
payload.formula_explanation: Tracks the absolute number of new subscribers acquired each quarter, broken down by subscription type. Shows both total new acquisitions and how many opted into email and SMS marketing.
how_to_read: X-axis shows quarters over the past 3 years
Y-axis shows subscriber count
Blue line/area shows total new subscribers
Green line shows active email subscribers
Purple line shows active SMS subscribers
The gap between total and active indicates opt-out rate
decisions_answered: Is our customer acquisition growing or shrinking over time?
What is the opt-in rate for email and SMS marketing?
Are there seasonal patterns in customer acquisition?
How does current quarter acquisition compare to historical performance?
payload.narration_text:
This chart tracks new subscriber acquisition over the past three years by quarter.

There are three lines to watch: Blue shows total new subscribers - your raw acquisition volume. Green shows those who opted into email marketing. Purple shows SMS subscribers - typically a smaller but valuable segment.

The gap between total and active subscribers indicates your opt-out rate. A widening gap means more people are declining marketing communications.

Use this to answer: Is acquisition growing? What are your opt-in rates? Are there seasonal patterns? Compare current performance to historical benchmarks to gauge marketing effectiveness.

payload.formula_text:
ROAS = Revenue / Spend
CPC = Spend / Clicks
CPA = Spend / Conversions
CPM = (Spend / Impressions) × 1000
payload.formula_explanation: Consolidates key efficiency metrics by platform (Google Ads, Meta Ads) across quarters. Enables comparison of ad spend efficiency over time and between platforms.
how_to_read: Each row represents one platform for one quarter
ROAS is colored: green (>6x), yellow (4-6x), red (<4x)
CPA is colored: green (<$4), yellow ($4-6), red (>$6)
Compare rows across quarters to see efficiency trends
Sort by any column to rank platforms/quarters on that metric
decisions_answered: Which platform delivers better ROAS?
Is our efficiency improving or declining over time?
What is our cost per acquisition by platform?
How do CPC and CPM trends indicate competition and costs?
payload.narration_text:
This table shows key efficiency metrics for Google Ads and Meta Ads across quarters.

The core formulas: ROAS is revenue divided by spend. CPC is spend divided by clicks. CPA is spend divided by conversions. CPM is spend divided by impressions, times a thousand.

Color coding helps quick assessment: Green ROAS means strong returns over 6x. Yellow is moderate at 4 to 6x. Red signals concern under 4x. For CPA, lower is better - green is under 4 dollars.

Compare across quarters to spot trends. Are you getting more or less efficient over time? Compare platforms to see which delivers better returns. This informs budget allocation decisions for the next quarter.

payload.formula_text:
Growth Opportunity = Current YTD Revenue × 0.15
Projected Total = Current Revenue + Growth Opportunity
payload.formula_explanation: Shows current year-to-date revenue by channel plus a projected 15% growth opportunity. This conservative estimate helps identify which channels have the most room for scaling based on current performance.
how_to_read: Horizontal bars show revenue by channel
Solid portion represents current YTD revenue
Lighter/pattern portion shows estimated growth opportunity
Longer total bar length indicates higher total opportunity
Channels are ranked by current revenue (top = highest)
decisions_answered: Which channels have the largest absolute revenue opportunity?
Where should incremental marketing budget be allocated?
How does current performance map to growth potential?
Which channels are already maximized vs. have room to grow?
payload.narration_text:
This horizontal bar chart sizes market opportunity by channel.

The formula is simple: we take current year-to-date revenue and add a conservative 15% growth opportunity estimate. The solid bar portion is current revenue. The lighter portion shows potential upside.

Channels are ranked by current revenue, so top channels appear first. But don't just chase the biggest bars - consider the proportion of opportunity to current revenue.

This helps you decide: Where should you invest incremental budget? Which channels have untapped potential? A channel with a large opportunity segment relative to current revenue might be under-invested.

payload.formula_text: Seasonality Index = (Monthly Average Revenue / Overall Average Revenue) × 100
payload.formula_explanation: A seasonality index of 100 means average performance. Values above 100 indicate peak periods for that channel. Values below 100 indicate slow periods. This reveals when each channel performs best throughout the year.
how_to_read: Rows represent different marketing channels
Columns represent months (Jan through Dec)
Cell color intensity indicates performance: brighter = higher seasonality index
Values in cells show the exact seasonality index (100 = average)
Blue/darker cells indicate below-average months; green/brighter cells indicate peak months
decisions_answered: When are peak periods for each marketing channel?
Which months should we increase budget for specific channels?
Are there low-performing periods where budget could be reallocated?
How do seasonality patterns differ between channels?
payload.narration_text:
This heatmap reveals monthly seasonality patterns for each marketing channel over the past two years.

The seasonality index formula: monthly average revenue divided by overall average revenue, times 100. An index of 100 is average. Above 100 means peak performance. Below 100 means slow period.

Read it by looking at color intensity. Brighter cells are peak months. Darker cells are slow months. Each row is a channel, each column is a month.

This answers critical planning questions: When should you ramp up spend for each channel? When should you pull back? How do channel patterns differ? Use this to optimize quarterly budget allocation.

payload.formula_text: YoY Growth % = ((Current Year Revenue - Prior Year Revenue) / Prior Year Revenue) × 100
payload.formula_explanation: Each grouped bar compares the same quarter across multiple years. The YoY growth percentage shows how much each channel has grown or declined compared to the same quarter in the previous year.
how_to_read: Each cluster of bars represents one channel (e.g., Organic Search, Direct, Paid Search)
Different colored bars within a cluster represent different years for comparison
Taller bars indicate higher revenue for that channel in that year
Compare bar heights within a cluster to see year-over-year changes
Channels are sorted by current year revenue, highest to lowest
decisions_answered: Which marketing channels are growing or declining year-over-year?
How does this quarter compare to the same quarter in previous years?
Which channels deserve more investment based on growth trajectory?
Are there channels showing concerning YoY decline that need attention?
payload.narration_text:
This grouped bar chart shows revenue by channel across multiple years for the same quarter.

The formula for YoY growth is straightforward: current year revenue minus prior year revenue, divided by prior year revenue, times 100.

To read this chart: each cluster represents a marketing channel. The bars within each cluster show different years. Compare bar heights to see growth or decline.

Channels are sorted by current revenue, so top performers appear first. This helps answer: Which channels are growing? Which are declining? And where should you invest more or less based on performance trajectory?
